verses.text: Thou shalt not sacrifice an ox or sheep wherein is blemish, any evilfavouredness; for that is an abomination unto the LORD. Guidance: God reads what you brought. A blemished animal is a discount offering — and God names it 'abomination.' Worship that costs nothing is worth nothing. He receives the best you have or He sets your gift aside. The price of the sacrifice is part of its truth.

If there be found man or woman in thy gates which hath transgressed His covenant, gone served other gods — sun, moon, host of heaven, which I have not commanded — and it be told thee, enquired diligently, true and certain — bring them forth and stone them. At the mouth of two or three witnesses shall he be put to death; not at the mouth of one. The hands of the witnesses shall be first upon him. Guidance: One accuser is never enough. The law's first protection is against malicious slander. Two or three independent witnesses, and the accuser must throw the first stone — no anonymous denunciation here. Justice is public, slow, and witness-anchored.

If there arise a matter too hard for thee — between blood and blood, plea and plea, stroke and stroke — get thee up to the place the LORD shall choose. Come unto the priests the Levites, and the judge in those days; they shall shew thee the sentence. Thou shalt do according to the sentence; not decline to the right hand or the left. The man who will do presumptuously, that man shall die. Guidance: A supreme court at the central sanctuary. Hard cases get the careful eye of priests and the deliberating mind of the judge. Once they rule, you obey — no second-guessing. God designs systems where authority is real and humility is required.

When thou shalt say, I will set a king over me, like all the nations — thou shalt set him whom the LORD shall choose: one from among thy brethren, not a stranger. He shall not multiply horses, nor cause the people to return to Egypt for horses. Neither shall he multiply wives, that his heart turn not away: neither shall he greatly multiply silver and gold. Guidance: God permits a king but writes the job description. The three temptations of kingship are listed by name — horses (military empire), wives (foreign alliances and idolatry), gold (luxury and greed). Solomon would later break every one. The warnings are written before the throne is built.

When he sitteth upon the throne, he shall write him a copy of this law in a book: it shall be with him, he shall read therein all the days of his life: that he may learn to fear the LORD, keep all the words of this law. That his heart be not lifted up above his brethren. To the end that he may prolong his days in his kingdom. Guidance: The king's first job is to copy out the law by hand. The act itself was the discipleship — slow, manual, line by line. And the daily reading kept him a brother, not above his brothers. A throne without scripture grows brittle; a throne under scripture lasts.
